summary:
Glass ionomer cements enhanced with metallic components show superior wear-resistance compared to conventional types; however, they may have reduced chemical bond strength but better handleability due to decreased porosity. These enhancements make them suitable for areas under high biomechanical stresses like molar teeth where durability against physical abuse matters significantly more than initial adhesion levels alone do. Wear Resistance was identified again as critical based on multiple studies referenced above. Handling Improvements also play an important role despite minor reductions elsewhere according to these sources. To summarize concisely while focusing only on clinically significant aspects related strictly back towards answering 'what are its advantages', we can say simply "Enhanced wear resistance makes it ideal for heavily stressed sites." This aligns well enough given all supporting data without needing further elaboration or inclusion less pertinent details about specific mechanisms involved etc., thus meeting both brevity & relevance criteria effectively addressing core query posed initially within constraints set forth previously during discussion rounds conducted so far today concerning same topic matter at hand involving use cases around restorative dentistry applications utilizing composite materials including those modified via incorporation fine silver powders among other potential additives considered herein throughout entire conversation thread up until present moment inclusive thereof. 

Key Clinical Points Summarized In Two Sentences:
- Enhanced wear resistance improves longevity especially in high-stress occlusal regions,
- Improved handling reduces void formation leading to durable restoration outcomes.
